metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `compute_validity`'s plan-review branch must add a `manifest_regver(manifest) != registry_version(repo_root)` staleness check (returning `stale-regver`) — not just thread `repo_root` through the function.
findings.finding: compute_validity's plan-review branch (lines 513–570) currently performs no regver comparison. The plan threads `repo_root` into `compute_validity` but never states that `compute_validity` must also ADD a `manifest_regver(manifest) != registry_version(repo_root)` staleness check. Without that check being added, threading `repo_root` alone achieves nothing: `claim_gate_check` → `compute_validity` will still certify a stale attestation when only the overlay changes.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's 'What' section, add: '`compute_validity` plan-review branch additionally checks `manifest_regver(manifest) != registry_version(repo_root)` and returns `{"valid": False, "verdict": "stale-regver"}` on mismatch, analogous to the checks already in `drift_refresh_candidate` and `remediation_mode_candidate`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `disabled_builtins` is a dedicated signed manifest line (requiring a new `disabled_builtins: [G3,G5]` line in `build_manifest` + a `manifest_disabled_builtins()` parser) or lives only in `coverage` (which is not directly part of the HMAC-signed manifest lines); the plan currently says 'signed manifest' but the mechanism only puts it in coverage.
findings.finding: The plan says `disabled_builtins` is 'folded into the signed manifest' but `build_manifest()` only reads `coverage.counts` from the verdict; it emits no `disabled_builtins:` line. The plan doesn't specify a manifest line format or a new parser (`manifest_disabled_builtins`). An implementer can satisfy AC3 by writing the field only to `coverage` (not to the manifest lines), yet the signed HMAC would not bind it — a discrepancy between the plan's claim and the actual signing boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact overlay schema field/sentinel that marks a built-in as disabled (e.g. `"disabled": true` in the routing entry, or a reserved `block_threshold` value) and confirm `_validate_routing_entry` + `effective_criteria` will be updated to recognize and propagate it before `coverage.disabled_builtins` can be populated.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a built-in criterion can be 'disabled' and that disabled built-ins will appear in `coverage.disabled_builtins`, but the existing overlay routing schema (validated by `_validate_routing_entry`) has no `disabled` flag or sentinel value defined — it only validates `exec`, `block_threshold`, and `default_posture`. The mechanism by which a built-in is marked disabled (e.g. a new `"disabled": true` routing key, a special `block_threshold` sentinel, or exclusion from `effective_criteria`) is unspecified, meaning neither the schema validator nor the `effective_criteria` / `load_criteria` call chain currently supports this. This is a load-bearing unverified capability assertion for AC3.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('A disabled built-in appears in `coverage.disabled_builtins` in the signed manifest') and ## Scope ('Files: attest.py … coverage field')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how a built-in is marked 'disabled' in the overlay (e.g. a `disabled: true` routing field or a sentinel exec value), add that detection/enumeration to `effective_routing`/`effective_criteria`, and name this as an explicit sub-task or dependency of AC3 — since ADR 0015 defers the disable mechanism to a later story and no such code exists today.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 ('A disabled built-in appears in coverage.disabled_builtins in the signed manifest') depends on a built-in-disable mechanism in the overlay, but that mechanism is explicitly deferred in ADR 0015 ('disable (a later story)') and has no implementation in registry.py — effective_routing() can re-tune a built-in's routing keys but has no 'disabled: true' field, no code path to detect/enumerate disabled built-ins, and no signal for attest.py to read. The plan treats this capability as immediately available without naming the missing prerequisite.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-step to AC3: '`registry.py` recognises a `"disabled": true` routing key (or equivalent sentinel) on an un-prefixed built-in id, excludes it from `effective_criteria`, and exposes `disabled_builtins(repo_root)` → list[str]; `attest.py` calls this at sign-time and populates `coverage["disabled_builtins"]`.' Alternatively, explicitly depend this story on the disable-mechanism story if that is separately tracked.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the overlay schema field that marks a built-in as disabled (e.g. `{"exec": "DISABLED"}` or `{"disabled": true}`) and specify how `effective_routing()` / `effective_criteria()` handle it so that `disabled_builtins` can be collected deterministically.
findings.finding: The overlay schema has no defined `disabled: true` sentinel to distinguish a 'disabled' built-in from a 're-tuned' one, yet AC3 requires collecting the disabled set to populate `disabled_builtins`. The plan says 'disabling a criterion' invalidates attestations, but the overlay merge code (`effective_routing()`) has no `disabled` concept — it only merges routing keys, never marks a criterion as disabled.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the manifest-line encoding for `disabled_builtins` (e.g. a `_DISABLED_PREFIX = 'disabled:'` constant + a `manifest_disabled_builtins(manifest)` reader) co-located with the other manifest parsers in `attest.py`, and specify it in the plan.
findings.finding: `disabled_builtins` is referenced as a `coverage` dict field 'folded into the signed manifest' (AC3/What) but no manifest-line serialization format, prefix constant, or reader function is defined — the plan specifies the field name but not the encoding protocol needed by `build_manifest()` and any future `manifest_disabled_builtins()` parser.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'A `disabled: <space-separated-ids>` line is appended to the manifest by `build_manifest()` when `coverage.disabled_builtins` is non-empty; a `manifest_disabled_builtins(manifest) -> list[str]` reader parses it back (empty list when absent — expand-contract).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly what the `.rebar/` overlay hash covers: file filter (e.g. `**/*.yaml`), traversal order (sorted), hash algorithm, and what is concatenated (path+content or content-only), so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies WHAT is hashed from .rebar/ — which files, which extensions, what traversal order, what happens to subdirectories — leaving the executor to make a load-bearing design choice that directly determines invalidation correctness.
findings.location: What section / AC1: 'registry_version(repo_root) hashes the .rebar/ overlay'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'hash is a SHA-256 of sorted(relative_path + SHA-256(content)) for all files matching `*.yaml` (or the overlay format) under .rebar/, ignoring __pycache__ and .DS_Store; missing directory ⇒ empty string contributor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting (a) the dual-source semantics of `registry_version` (packaged routing + overlay), (b) the `disabled_builtins` manifest field contract, and (c) the expand-contract staleness policy for old attestations.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel architectural contracts — (1) `registry_version` now incorporates overlay state in addition to packaged routing, changing the semantics of the version token relied on by all gate paths; (2) a new `disabled_builtins` field is added to the signed manifest with an expand-contract policy — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc change is mentioned to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-registry-version-overlay-and-disabled-builtins.md` covering: the motivation for hashing the overlay into `registry_version`, the expand-contract rule (absent field → stale, not crash), and guidance that future manifest field additions must follow the same pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan which `compute_validity()` sub-check enforces AC4, what the stale verdict string is, and how 'old attestation lacks `disabled_builtins`' is detected (e.g. compare `manifest_disabled_builtins(manifest)` against `current_disabled_set(repo_root)` — mismatch → stale).
findings.finding: The decision rule for AC4 ('old attestation lacking `disabled_builtins` treated as stale, never a crash') is not co-located with the `compute_validity()` code path that must implement it: neither the trigger condition (manifest has no `disabled_builtins` line), the stale `verdict` string, nor which branch of `compute_validity()` applies this check is specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each modified caller (registry_version, claim_gate_check, compute_validity, drift_refresh_candidate, remediation_mode_candidate), add or update a unit test that passes a non-None repo_root pointing to a fixture .rebar/ directory and asserts the returned version changes when the overlay changes.
findings.finding: Five callers modified by this story (registry_version, claim_gate_check, compute_validity, drift_refresh_candidate, remediation_mode_candidate) have no corresponding modify-test work called out, leaving behavioral regressions in the threading and invalidation logic undetectable.
findings.location: Scope — Files: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py; What section listing callers
findings.suggested_fix: Create a pytest fixture that writes a minimal .rebar/ overlay to a tmp_path, call each modified function with repo_root=tmp_path, record the version, mutate the overlay file, call again, and assert the versions differ. Assert versions are equal when no overlay is present (regression guard for the 'unchanged overlay does not churn' AC).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (e.g., in `test_attest.py`) that presents a cert payload missing the `disabled_builtins` key to `claim_gate_check`/`compute_validity` and asserts it is treated as stale (re-review triggered, no exception raised).
findings.finding: AC4 ('old attestation lacking disabled_builtins treated as stale, never a crash') is a failure-path on a newly introduced user-facing flow (cert validation) and has no test work described anywhere in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the test suite, construct a cert dict that omits `coverage.disabled_builtins`, call `compute_validity(cert, ...)`, and assert the result is `STALE` (not an exception). Mirror with `claim_gate_check` integration path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the ticket to reflect actual scope (e.g., 'Attestation invalidation: registry_version overlay hashing + disabled_builtins coverage field') and/or add a bolded 'Out of scope: blocking policy (already shipped)' callout at the top of the body.
findings.finding: The title 'Blocking policy + attestation integration' implies blocking-policy work is being delivered, but the body explicitly states 'Blocking itself is NOT in scope (already shipped via threshold_for + kernel Pass-3).' An implementer reading the title alone would expect new blocking behavior, yet the plan delivers only attestation/invalidation wiring.
findings.location: Title vs. Scope section ('Blocking itself is NOT in scope')
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Attestation integration: registry_version(repo_root) overlay hashing, disabled_builtins coverage field, and claim-time invalidation'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note: validate the no-churn guarantee (overlay-absent → regver unchanged) as a prerequisite merge before threading repo_root through all callers, and validate expand-contract behavior against a sample old-cert before shipping the coverage-field schema change.
findings.finding: The plan has no explicit thin vertical slice or evidence-gated sequencing: all five ACs (hash change, threading, coverage field, expand-contract, verdict folding) are presented as a flat set with no identified riskiest piece to validate first. The riskiest unknowns — (1) whether overlay-absent path truly produces identical regver (no-churn guarantee) and (2) whether the expand-contract stale logic correctly handles all old-cert shapes — are not sequenced before the remaining ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 5 ACs listed without ordering or phasing)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to 'Why' or 'What' explaining why rolling the overlay bytes into `registry_version()` (reusing `_overlay_signature`, the already-existing content-hash) is preferred over a separate manifest field.
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why rolling the overlay into `registry_version()` (rather than, e.g., a dedicated `overlay-sig:` manifest field) is the right approach. The 'Why' section explains the bug; the 'What' section describes the mechanism; neither states WHY this approach is chosen.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Rolling the overlay into `registry_version()` reuses the existing `_overlay_signature()` content-hash already used as the `effective_routing` cache key, keeping regver as the single version-of-truth for any registry change — no new manifest field or parser needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the overlay is read atomically at claim time (e.g., sort and hash only committed/complete files, use a temp-file+rename write convention for overlay updates, or document that overlay writes are operator-serialized outside the claim path).
findings.finding: The plan hashes the `.rebar/` overlay at claim time via a filesystem read, but does not address atomicity: a concurrent overlay write (e.g., an operator activating/editing a criterion while a claim check is in flight) could cause the hash to be computed over a partially-written directory, producing an incorrect version token — either falsely matching a prior attestation or producing a spurious mismatch.
findings.location: ## What — registry_version(repo_root) hashing; ## Acceptance Criteria AC#1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'rebind' in AC5 (e.g., 'a project criterion entry whose id matches a built-in id is rejected at load time with an error') and specify where the enforcement check lives.
findings.finding: AC5 contains the constraint 'it can never rebind a built-in' but nowhere in the plan is 'rebind' defined or its enforcement mechanism described, making the criterion both ambiguous (E2) and unmeasurable (F1).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all files that contain `claim_gate_check`, `compute_validity`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, and `remediation_mode_candidate` in the Scope file list so the executor does not need to discover them.
findings.finding: The Scope file list names only `attest.py` and 'the claim-gate path', but the What section requires threading `repo_root` through `drift_refresh_candidate` and `remediation_mode_candidate`, which may live in different modules — leaving the executor to discover those files via code archaeology.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Files: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py ... the claim-gate path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body-level description of the 'cannot rebind a built-in' rule — specify WHERE the guard lives (e.g., overlay loader, registry merge, attestation writer), WHAT it checks, and HOW it signals failure, so AC5 is fully traceable to described work.
findings.finding: AC5 contains the sub-claim 'it can never rebind a built-in' but the plan body ('What', 'Scope') never describes the mechanism, rule, or guard that enforces this constraint — leaving a criterion sub-clause with no corresponding described work.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a bullet such as: 'During overlay merge, validate that no project criterion ID collides with a built-in registry key; raise a configuration error (not a silent override) if a collision is detected. This guard lives in [overlay loader / registry.merge].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC2's verify clause to use consistent terminology: replace 'regver == today' with a precise, unambiguous condition referencing the function and its expected output (e.g., 'registry_version(repo_root=None) returns the same value as when no overlay is present').
findings.finding: AC2 uses the shorthand 'regver' without defining it anywhere in the plan, creating a terminology inconsistency relative to the consistently-used 'registry_version' / 'overlay version' terminology elsewhere.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(regver == today when overlay absent)' with '(registry_version() returns the same hash as the packaged-routing-only baseline when no .rebar/ overlay exists)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
